claims_verified.claim: A write-time OSError (e.g. ENOSPC after O_CREAT|O_EXCL succeeded) leaves a permanent, uncleaned empty file at the intended slot
claims_verified.evidence: Live repro against note_server/writer.py: monkeypatched os.fdopen to raise OSError(28, 'No space left on device') after os.open succeeded. write_note('notes', ...) raised WriteError as expected, but os.listdir(d) afterward showed 'notes.md' (0 bytes) still present. A second, successful call to write_note(folder, 'notes', 'second attempt') then silently wrote to 'notes-2.md' instead of reusing/reporting 'notes.md', leaving the empty file behind permanently.

claims_verified.claim: No test asserts directory state after a write-time OS failure
claims_verified.evidence: tests/note_server/test_writer.py:78-92 (test_write_note_wraps_write_time_os_error) only asserts pytest.raises(WriteError) and that the path string is absent from the exception message; it never inspects tmp_path's contents after the failure, so the orphaned empty file goes unnoticed

claims_verified.claim: TOTP window logic, project_name allowlist, and auth-then-validate-then-write ordering in handle_save_note are implemented as specified
claims_verified.evidence: note_server/totp.py:62-90, note_server/writer.py:12,26-28, note_server/server.py:40-51 read as spec'd; no defect found on these paths

findings.summary: write_note creates the target file via O_CREAT|O_EXCL before writing content; if the subsequent write fails (os.fdopen/handle.write OSError, e.g. ENOSPC), the now-empty file is never removed, permanently occupying the intended filename slot with no content and no record that anything went wrong.
findings.failure_scenario: save_note(project_name='notes', ...) hits a transient OS failure while writing content (disk fills up mid-write, IO error, etc.). The caller gets 'Error: write failed - the note could not be saved.' but 'notes.md' now exists on disk, empty, forever - no cleanup path exists anywhere in write_note. If the caller reasonably retries the identical request expecting it to either succeed at 'notes.md' or fail again, the retry instead silently succeeds by writing to 'notes-2.md' (FileExistsError branch treats the empty derelict file as a legitimate prior note), permanently orphaning the empty file and silently renaming the user's note away from the name they asked for and expect. Reproduced live: after a simulated write-time OSError, a second write_note call for the same project_name returned 'notes-2.md' while 'notes.md' (0 bytes) remained in the folder untouched and unexplained.

test_assessment: AC1-AC4, AC7 are well covered. AC5 (no-clobber dedup) and AC6 (no path leak) are tested for the happy path and for the auth/validation/create-time-OSError branches, but no test exercises directory state after a write-time (post-create) OS failure - which is exactly the branch where the bug above lives. The two write-time-OSError tests (test_write_note_wraps_write_time_os_error, test_write_error_response_is_clean_and_hides_folder_path) both only assert on the exception/response text, never on what got left in tmp_path.
residual_risks: Every transient write failure after file creation (ENOSPC, revoked permissions mid-write, disk I/O error) leaves permanent empty-file debris in the notes folder and silently shifts subsequent writes to the next -N suffix, which will accumulate unnoticed on a long-running VPS process.

config.py's non-writable-folder case (noted by rw1) is a special case of this same gap: the first write attempt for a given project_name will always leave behind an empty file before the caller ever finds out the folder isn't writable.
